learn.title: Solar PV and the Photoelectric Effect
learn.intro: Solar PV panels convert sunlight into electricity using the photoelectric effect, where photons knock electrons loose in silicon atoms. This process enables clean, decentralized energy generation, reducing reliance on fossil fuels and supporting global decarbonization goals.
learn.c1_text: Photons from sunlight hit solar cells, dislodging electrons in silicon layers. This creates an electric current, akin to a waterfall generating hydroelectric power. One square meter of solar panels can produce ~1 kW of power under ideal conditions.
learn.c2_text: The generated direct current (DC) is converted to alternating current (AC) via inverters. This AC powers homes and grids, similar to how a transformer adjusts voltage for safe household use. Inverters typically operate at 90-95% efficiency.
learn.c3_text: India's solar capacity reached 74 GW (2024), driven by rooftop and utility-scale projects. The PM Surya Ghar Yojana aims to install 40 GW of rooftop solar by 2030, leveraging the photoelectric effect for rural electrification.
learn.stat2: Solar panels convert ~20% of sunlight into electricity

learn.title: Demand Response for Grid Balance
learn.intro: Large energy users can adjust consumption during peak demand, stabilizing grids and reducing reliance on fossil fuel backups. This practice lowers costs and supports renewable integration.
learn.c1_text: Industrial users reduce load during peak hours (e.g., 5-9 PM), akin to a thermostat adjusting HVAC use. A 100 MW reduction equals power from 100 turbines, easing grid stress.
learn.c2_text: Smart systems automatically adjust operations using grid signals. For example, factories might pause non-essential processes, similar to a smart thermostat adjusting heating based on occupancy.
learn.c3_text: India's demand response market is growing, with 1.5 GW of registered capacity (2023). The UDAY scheme incentivizes industries to cut peak demand, reducing grid strain and fossil fuel use.
learn.stat3: Peak demand reduction: 10-15% via DR programs

learn.title: Hydrogen Fuel Cells in Vehicles
learn.intro: Hydrogen fuel cells generate electricity through a chemical reaction between hydrogen and oxygen, emitting only water. They offer zero-emission transport solutions for heavy-duty and long-haul vehicles.
learn.c1_text: Hydrogen reacts with oxygen in a fuel cell stack, producing electricity and water. A single fuel cell generates ~1 V, with stacks combining hundreds of cells. Efficiency exceeds 60%, outperforming internal combustion engines.
learn.c2_text: Hydrogen refueling stations compress hydrogen to 700 bar, similar to gasoline pumps. A 5-minute fill-up provides 400-600 km range, enabling long-distance travel without range anxiety.
learn.c3_text: India aims for 1 million fuel cell vehicles by 2030. NITI Aayog targets 50 GW renewable hydrogen by 2030, with pilot projects in Delhi and Mumbai using hydrogen buses.
learn.stat2: Fuel cell efficiency: 60% vs. 20% for ICEs

learn.title: Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)
learn.intro: LCOE standardizes cost comparisons across energy sources by calculating the average cost per kWh over a project's lifetime. It reveals renewables' cost competitiveness against fossil fuels.
learn.c1_text: LCOE = (Initial Cost + O&M + Financing) / (Energy Output × Lifetime). For solar, this includes panel costs, inverter replacement, and land. Coal LCOE is ~$60/MWh, while solar is ~$40/MWh.
learn.c2_text: Solar LCOE dropped 85% since 2010 due to falling panel prices. Wind and battery storage follow similar trends, making renewables cheaper than new coal or gas plants in most regions.
learn.c3_text: India's solar LCOE is ₹2.8-3.5/kWh (2023), below grid tariffs. The National Solar Mission targets 500 GW by 2030, with auctions driving record-low bids like ₹2.44/kWh in 2023.

learn.title: Geothermal Energy Basics
learn.intro: Geothermal energy taps Earth's internal heat for power and direct heating. This reliable, low-carbon source operates 24/7, complementing intermittent renewables like solar and wind.
learn.c1_text: Drill wells into hot rock formations to circulate water, creating steam that drives turbines. A 100 MW geothermal plant uses ~1,000 tons of rock daily, equivalent to 10,000 barrels of oil annually.
learn.c2_text: Hot water from geothermal reservoirs heats buildings and greenhouses. Iceland meets 90% of heating needs this way, while Kenya uses it for horticulture, boosting agricultural output.
learn.c3_text: India has 340 MW identified potential, with projects in Gujarat and Himachal Pradesh. The Geothermal Mission aims to drill 12 exploratory wells by 2025, targeting 1 GW by 2030.

learn.title: Solar Panel Types Compared
learn.intro: Monocrystalline, polycrystalline, and thin-film solar panels differ in efficiency, cost, and durability. Choosing the right type impacts solar project performance and ROI.
learn.c1_text: Monocrystalline panels use single-crystal silicon, achieving 20-22% efficiency. They perform better in low light and have longer lifespans (30+ years), like premium sports cars for solar systems.
learn.c2_text: Polycrystalline panels use fragmented silicon, offering 15-17% efficiency at lower cost. They’re like economy cars—reliable but less efficient. Ideal for budget-conscious rooftop installations.
learn.c3_text: Thin-film panels (e.g., CdTe, CIGS) are lightweight and flexible, suiting curved surfaces. India's Bhadla Solar Park uses thin-film modules, achieving 1 GW capacity with lower land costs.
